principle: Repeat one visual element throughout the entire design to create unity — repetition is the difference between a collection of items and a coherent design
explanation: X.ui|repetition_unity: scattered_elements == collection_not_design; repetitive_element: bold_font+thick_rule+bullet_shape+color+spatial_relationship+ANY_recognizable_element; function: ties_disparate_parts_into_one_cohesive_piece; test: remove_repetitive_element -> does_design_fall_apart? -> it_was_working; NO:each_section_with_different_visual_treatment NO:repetition_only_via_color(too_subtle); eg: three_separate_card_sections_no_connection >> bold_typeface_repeated_across_all_sections_creates_unity

principle: Identify your existing consistencies and amplify them into deliberate graphic statements — upgrading a 14pt bold headline to 16pt ultra-bold turns passive consistency into active design
explanation: X.ui|amplify_consistency: existing_consistency: headlines_all_same_size == weak_passive_repetition; amplify: take_consistent_element -> push_stronger+more_dramatic; eg: 14pt_Times_Bold_headlines >> 16pt_Mikado_Ultra_headlines -> more_visual_interest+stronger_organization; benefit: not_only_interesting_page_but_increased_clarity; start: find_existing_repetitions -> strengthen_them -> THEN_add_new_ones; NO:starting_with_new_elements_before_strengthening_existing NO:subtle_weak_repetition_that_doesnt_register; eg: 12pt_bold_headlines >> 18pt_black_ultra_bold_headlines

principle: In multi-page documents, use repetition so that any two pages are instantly recognizable as belonging to the same publication — a page 13 with no elements from page 3 breaks cohesion
explanation: X.ui|multipage_repetition: multi_page_without_repetition: page_13_looks_unrelated_to_page_3 -> publication_loses_cohesive_look; repetitive_elements_across_pages: same_header_font+same_rule_position+same_column_structure+same_color_accent; test: open_to_random_page -> is_it_instantly_obvious_this_belongs_to_the_same_doc? NO:different_treatment_per_chapter_with_no_throughline NO:repetition_only_on_first_page; eg: each_chapter_has_different_header_style >> consistent_header_rule+typeface+color_across_all_chapters

principle: Carry repetitive visual elements across all brand touchpoints so a business card, letterhead, and envelope are instantly recognizable as belonging to the same person or company
explanation: X.ui|brand_repetition: mismatched_stationery: business_card_looks_unrelated_to_letterhead -> undermines_credibility; solution: identify_2-3_strong_visual_elements(typeface_combination+color+graphic_element) -> apply_consistently_across_all_materials; test: person_who_got_your_card_last_week_should_recognize_your_letter_today; NO:redesigning_each_piece_independently NO:same_name_different_look_per_medium; eg: different_font+color_on_card_vs_letterhead >> shared_bold_typeface+accent_color+logo_across_card+letterhead+envelope

principle: Use strong repetition to earn the right to break the pattern — only a consistently designed publication can use a surprise element that readers will recognize as intentionally special
explanation: X.ui|repetition_then_contrast: surprise_on_inconsistent_page == more_noise; surprise_on_consistent_page == emphasis; mechanism: establish_strong_repetition_first -> reader_learns_the_pattern -> break_it_once_for_critical_item -> reader_notices; example: everything_in_serif_except_one_callout_in_bold_sans -> callout_reads_as_intentional_highlight; NO:breaking_pattern_before_establishing_it NO:so_many_surprises_the_pattern_never_forms; eg: inconsistent_design_with_random_highlights >> consistent_design+single_deliberate_break_for_most_important_element
